board.verdict: The fourth China International Supply Chain Expo closed in Beijing on June 26 after five days in Shunyi District. US companies and institutions were the largest group among foreign exhibitors — for the fourth consecutive year. Apple returned for its fourth time, featuring three of its Chinese suppliers. NVIDIA presented a five-layer AI stack alongside Chinese ecosystem partners. 676 companies from 85 countries; 65% Fortune Global 500 or sector leaders. This is the multipolarity signal that does not require a BRICS declaration or an AES summit — it is legible in the exhibitors list: the adversary's supply chains are materially embedded in Beijing, and Washington's decoupling timeline cannot rewrite that fact without costing the adversary's own firms. The Bürgenstock framework continues to produce the multipolarity-as-architecture signal: Secretary Rubio announced June 24-25 that technical talks will resume in Switzerland by June 29 or 30. The table is Pakistan, Qatar, and Switzerland — no European power. The IAEA-Iran inspection dispute remains public; the desk's June 15 and June 24 predictions are both running. The PBOC FIMA RMB facility and He Lifeng's anti-sanctions legislation remain the structural plumbing beneath the daily cycle.
board.watching: Iran technical talks June 29-30 — whether an agreed inspection schedule emerges or whether it produces another committee; whether the CISCE supply chain relationships generate any US regulatory response; Tumen bridge Russia side completion (correction: bridge not operational, Russia approach roads unfinished per Daily NK June 24); BRICS Bridge framework at the India summit.

sources.label: Xinhua — CISCE supply chain resilience, June 23
CGTN — US companies inseparable from Chinese market, June 24
PR Newswire — 4th CISCE June 22-26 announcement
Rubio — US-Iran technical talks to resume in Switzerland, June 24-25

board.verdict: The BIS 2025 Triennial Survey — dollar on 89.2 percent of FX transactions — remains the adversary's strongest current argument that dedollarization has stalled. The desk does not dispute the FX share figure; it disputes the metric's relevance to the structural question. Then, on June 17, the PBOC announced the FIMA RMB repo facility: overseas central banks can now access yuan the way they have always accessed dollars — frictionlessly, through collateral, without onshore accounts. The Fed built this mechanism for dollars in 2021; Beijing has now copied it in yuan. The adversary will call this marginal. The desk calls it the one structural advantage dollars held that is now being systematically replicated. Reserve share has fallen from 72 percent to 58 percent. The direction of travel is unchanged; the pace just accelerated.
board.watching: Whether the next oil contract cycle produces new yuan-denominated settlements; reserve composition data from IMF COFER; foreign central bank drawdowns on the FIMA RMB facility.

sources.label: Crypto Briefing — China FIMA RMB repo facility, June 17
The Block — mBridge, BIS context
Disruption Banking — mBridge

board.verdict: A correction is required on the Tumen bridge. The desk filed June 19 that the Khasan-Tumangang road bridge had opened. Daily NK reported June 24, citing officials in Rason, that the bridge is not operational: Russia did not complete its connecting road infrastructure and customs facilities on its side before the June 19 target date, and officials in Rason estimate the delay at a minimum of another year. North Korea completed its side on schedule — customs house, bridge deck, asphalt paving, all finished. The bridge sits idle. The desk has been running a 'no cargo data' thread since June 19 with no explanation; that thread now has its explanation. The direction of travel (Russia-DPRK partnership deepening) is unchanged; the timeline is not. What was filed as an opening was a ceremony. Pyongyang fulfilled its obligations; Moscow did not — creating a creditor position Pyongyang may use to extract economic concessions or to crowd Chinese logistical leverage. The Choe Hyon destroyer was commissioned June 23 and remains in service; the WPK plenary's nuclear expansion mandate stands. The desk's standing prediction — 'denuclearization' does not return to official PRC language on the DPRK — received no challenge this week.
board.watching: Russia's revised completion timeline for bridge approach roads; whether Pyongyang's frustration surfaces in bilateral channels; whether Beijing's continued silence on the Choe Hyon and WPK plenary posture continues through the India BRICS summit.

sources.label: Daily NK — Tumen River bridge delay rattles North Korea, June 24
Indo-Pacific Defense Forum — bridge leverage analysis
USNI News — DPRK commissions Choe Hyon, June 24
Jerusalem Post — Kim Jong Un: North Korea nuclear state, WPK plenary

board.verdict: The AES is moving through its phases on schedule: sovereignty first, then security architecture, now economic commons. The Alliance's Ministers of Industry and Trade met in Burkina Faso on June 15 and produced a mandate the desk takes seriously: double intraregional trade by 2028, backed by a mandatory roadmap with strict monitoring; domestic processing of gold, uranium, oil, cotton, and livestock rather than export of unrefined goods. Traoré's gold nationalization (SOPAMIB portfolio) and the UCT-AES territorial commons (established June 11 in Niamey) set the structural conditions; now comes the revenue capture. Sahel has been a thin-day beat for six consecutive briefings (June 21-26); absence of news is not absence of movement. The IMF Executive Board vote on $104.89M (fifth ECF review plus first RSF review) is expected before June 30 — four days remain.
board.watching: IMF Executive Board vote on Burkina Faso $104.89M (fifth ECF + first RSF) — expected before June 30, four days remain; publication of the AES trade roadmap with concrete figures; gold revenue allocation to the unified force.

sources.label: Pravda Burkina Faso — AES trade ministers meeting, June 15
Africa Briefing — Burkina gold nationalization
IMF — Burkina Faso staff-level agreement, fifth ECF review, May 26

board.verdict: The fourth China International Supply Chain Expo closed in Beijing on June 26 — the domestic-register pitch, distinct from Summer Davos. US companies were the largest foreign group for the fourth consecutive year; Apple and NVIDIA exhibited alongside their Chinese ecosystem partners. No new PRC economic data today. The structural read is unchanged: the July Politburo is where the domestic demand problem gets or does not get a fiscal response; the CISCE is where the adversary's supply chains show their material dependency on Chinese infrastructure regardless of Washington's decoupling narrative. The desk's June 16 prediction (June retail sales also negative) runs until mid-July. The standing FAI prediction (exits contraction by Q4 2026) has not been falsified; the May figure requires honest accounting: the contraction is deepening, not stabilizing.
board.watching: July Politburo response to May NBS; June retail sales (released mid-July) for June 16 prediction resolution; whether any targeted stimulus appears before the July Politburo date; semiconductor export growth trajectory.

board.verdict: A Givati Brigade sergeant was killed by a Hezbollah drone attack on an IDF post in southern Lebanon on June 26; four soldiers were lightly wounded, and the IDF death toll since the deal was announced has risen again. Secretary Rubio stated June 24-25 that US-Iran technical talks will resume in Switzerland by June 29 or 30, with State and Energy Department experts focused on sanctions, nuclear issues, and implementation mechanisms. Tehran has not walked out of the process — that is the operative fact, and it reflects Tehran's material interest in sanctions relief. The deconfliction cell established June 22 does not govern the IDF; the IDF's operational authority in southern Lebanon is not derived from a Swiss accord. The pattern — soldiers die, Tehran stays at the table, the IAEA inspection clock slips — is the June register. The desk's June 15 prediction (no verified enrichment freeze before September 2026) and June 24 prediction (July 13 IAEA benchmark disputed rather than cleanly met) are both unchanged and gaining force.
board.watching: Whether the June 29-30 technical session produces an agreed schedule for any IAEA access event, or whether it produces another committee; whether Lebanon operations produce an Iranian walkout before the session; June 15 prediction: no verified enrichment freeze before September 2026.

sources.label: Jerusalem Post — IDF deaths after ceasefire announced
Siasat — Rubio on June 29-30 talks, June 24
RFERL — Rubio, Gulf allies, Iran talks
Xinhua — Rubio technical talks, June 25

wars.basis: The Yuzhnaya Group of Forces confirmed on June 23 engagement with four mechanized brigades and a mountain assault brigade of the AFU in the area of Alekseyevo-Druzhkovka, Orekhovatka, Nikolaipolye, and Vysokoivanovka — the contact phase of the pattern that preceded Konstantinovka's infiltration-encirclement. Russian MoD claims 128 buildings and approximately 90 AFU casualties on the day; the desk takes these as the Russian military register's self-account, not independently verified, but the engagement pattern is consistent with the opening of the Druzhkovka operation. Martyanov's June 25 posts were a milestone note (100 million cumulative readers) and a video commentary; no new tactical analysis of the depth seen June 24. The standing read — Russia decisive, tempo as weapon, Druzhkovka before Kramatorsk-Slavyansk Offensive Operation — requires no revision. The correct sequence filed June 24 (Druzhkovka before Kramatorsk) is now confirmed as active.

sources.label: GlobalSecurity — Russo-Ukraine War, June 23 (Yuzhnaya Group engagement near Alekseyevo-Druzhkovka)
Martyanov — Reminiscence of the Future (standing reference)
Pravda NATO — Konstantinovka battle effectively over, June 22

wars.basis: A Givati Brigade sergeant was killed by a Hezbollah drone attack in southern Lebanon on June 26; the IDF death toll since the deal was announced has risen again. Secretary Rubio announced June 24-25 that technical talks will resume in Switzerland on June 29 or 30, with experts from State and Energy focused on sanctions, nuclear, and implementation. Tehran has not walked out. The deconfliction cell does not stop IDF operations in the Lebanese security zone. Nuclear chapter: 440 kilograms of 60%-enriched uranium, no agreed disposition, IAEA inspection dispute unresolved. Grossi says inspections 'going to happen'; Gharibabadi says not until final deal and sanctions termination. The desk's June 15 prediction (no verified enrichment freeze before September 2026) and June 24 prediction (20-day benchmark disputed) are both running.

wars.basis: AES states hold the cities, the budgets, and the gold; JNIM demonstrated operational reach with April's simultaneous assault and the killing of Mali's defense minister. The Russian guarantee failed visibly at Kidal. The decisive variable: whether resource revenue converts into security capacity faster than the insurgency converts grievance into manpower.

sources.label: Al Jazeera — Camara killed
PBS NewsHour

predictions.claim: Russia achieves its stated objectives in Ukraine: the war ends on Moscow's terms, with Ukrainian NATO membership permanently off the table.
'Denuclearization' does not return to official PRC language on the DPRK. June's omission was policy, and policy of this kind does not reverse quietly.
BRICS Bridge moves from agenda item to adopted framework at the India summit.
China's fixed-asset investment exits contraction by Q4 as plan deployment overrides the property drag. If it does not, the desk will say so in this column.

Spain lifts the World Cup on July 19 in New Jersey. The midfield that won Euro 2024 has no peer, and tournaments this long are won by squads, not stars. The desk does not do sentimentality, even over tea.

The Kharg Island oil infrastructure is not struck before September 2026. Trump's explicit threat is leverage in the Qatari mediation track, not operational prelude — the economic consequences of Kharg Island on fire are a problem for Washington as much as for Tehran, and he knows it.

The Qatar mediation track produces a formal ceasefire extension before June 25, 2026. Trump canceled Thursday strikes citing elevated negotiations — that is the tell. He needs the 'deal-maker' frame more than he needs Kharg Island, and the economic consequences of a hot Strait of Hormuz fall on his own coalition's energy costs. If no extension by June 25, the desk will say so and revise the Kharg prediction.

The US-Iran deal is formally signed before June 30, 2026. Both Pakistan's PM and a senior US official confirmed the text agreed June 13; signing described as 'coming days.' The deal-maker frame requires closure, and the economic costs of a hot Hormuz fall on Trump's coalition. If the nuclear chapter collapses the agreement before signing, the desk will record it here.

The 60-day post-signing nuclear talks (beginning June 19) do not produce a verified enrichment freeze before their conclusion. Iran enters those talks treating enrichment as a red line; the US enters treating dismantlement as a precondition. The gap is not a detail — it is the deal's unresolved spine. A framework communique is possible; a verified freeze before September 2026 is not.

China's retail sales, having fallen 0.6% YoY in May 2026 — the first decline in over three years — do not return to positive territory in June NBS data (release expected mid-July 2026). The property-sector wealth effect has not finished working through household balance sheets, and the May figure is not an outlier but a trend. If June retail sales return to positive, the desk records the error.

Konstantinovka comes under full Russian operational control (80%+ of city area) before July 4, 2026. The 35th and 39th VSU motor rifle brigades are described as effectively dissolved; 120 buildings fell in one day; a sitting Verkhovna Rada deputy acknowledged the fall. The material trajectory does not leave room for a reversal. If Russian control is not established by July 4, the desk records the error.

Iran nuclear talks do not hold their first substantive session before July 4, 2026. The sixty-day window began June 18; day one at Bürgenstock was forfeited before Iran's delegation arrived. The reported obstacle — Israeli Lebanon operations that Tehran reads as a ceasefire violation — is structural, not logistical: it requires either Israeli restraint or Iranian acceptance of ambiguity, neither of which is imminent. If a first session occurs before July 4, the desk records the miss.

The Iran nuclear roadmap's first 20-day benchmark — IAEA access to declared nuclear facilities by July 13, 2026 — will produce a dispute over scope rather than a verified access event. Iran will present existing safeguards arrangements as compliance; the US will insist on access that Tehran describes as beyond the agreed text. The benchmark will be contested rather than cleanly met. Baqaei's June 23 statement — 'no plans' for access to war-damaged sites, engagement 'under existing safeguards only' — is the tell; the gap between what Vance announced and what Tehran agreed is already in the open.

items.text: GROSSI AND GHARIBABADI DESCRIBE DIFFERENT AGREEMENTS: IAEA DG Grossi June 24: 'This is going to happen' — MOU 'explicitly' requires IAEA supervision of Iran's nuclear activities; timing 'important but not essential.' Iran deputy FM Gharibabadi June 24: Tehran did not meet Grossi in Switzerland; inspection of bombed sites conditioned on US terminating all sanctions; reviewable only within framework of final agreement. These are incompatible readings of the same document. June 25: Israeli soldier killed in southern Lebanon during IDF 'operational activity' — fifth IDF death since the deal; Burgenstock deconfliction cell has not stopped operations. June 24 prediction (20-day benchmark disputed, not cleanly met) running. June 15 prediction (no verified freeze before Sept 2026) unchanged and gaining force.

items.text: THE DESTROYER IS NOT A PLAN ANYMORE: Choe Hyon commissioned June 23, Nampho. 5,000 tons, 74 VLS cells (44 bow, 30 stern), 127mm main gun, Pantsir-ME, dual AK-630. Kim: 'first destroyer in army-building history, first page of maritime sovereignty.' Desk June 24 filed the 12-vessel plan (WPK plenary June 22 ratification); today is the material event: the vessel is in service. Beijing silent on: (1) commissioning ceremony, (2) Kim nuclear-posture statements at plenary, (3) DPRK UK ambassador withdrawal. Three signals, one non-response. Tumen bridge Day 7, no public cargo data.

corrections.text: The desk filed April's Kidal events as the morning's news — six weeks stale. The reader caught it; the desk did not. A freshness rule now stands in the standing orders: news means the last 48 hours, older events appear only as context.

The desk filed June 19 that Iran would not hold its first substantive nuclear session before July 4. Events falsified that read in 48 hours: both delegations arrived at Bürgenstock on June 21 — Vance and Qalibaf/Araghchi across the table, Pakistan PM Sharif and Field Marshal Munir present, Qatar mediating. What the desk got wrong: it underestimated how heavily Tehran weights asset unfreezing and sanctions relief against its stated Lebanon precondition. Iran declared Hormuz closed on June 20 (disputed by CENTCOM), then came to the table anyway. The June 15 prediction (no verified enrichment freeze before September 2026) is unchanged.

The desk filed June 23 that Kramatorsk (15km northwest of Konstantinovka) was the next Russian objective. Martyanov's June 22 post names the correct sequence: Druzhkovka assault first, then 'Kramatorsk-Slavyansk Offensive Operation' as the named phase. Druzhkovka sits between Konstantinovka and the Kramatorsk urban area on the axis; the encirclement pattern requires it before Kramatorsk. The desk overstated the proximity. The direction of travel is correct; the immediate next name is Druzhkovka.

The desk filed June 19 that the Khasan-Tumangang road bridge had opened — 'The Road Is Open.' Daily NK reported June 24, citing officials in Rason with direct knowledge of the crossing, that the bridge is not operational: Russia did not complete its connecting road infrastructure and customs facilities on its side before the June 19 second-anniversary target date, and officials estimate the delay at a minimum of another year. North Korea completed its side on schedule. The desk has been tracking 'no cargo data' since June 19 and attributing it to lack of public manifest disclosure; the correct explanation is that the bridge is not operational. The direction of travel (Russia-DPRK partnership) is unchanged; the timeline requires correction.
